words.definition_en: to state that something is true or that someone has done something wrong or illegal even though this has not been proved
words.definition_vi: cáo cáo, khẳng định mà chưa có bằng chứng
words.example: The defense representative alleged that the police had used force to extract a confession.

words.definition_en: not clear or decided, or having more than one possible meaning
words.definition_vi: mơ hồ, đa nghĩa, không rõ ràng
words.example: The phrasing of the law is somewhat ambiguous.

words.definition_en: to state firmly that something is true
words.example: He asserted that he was not guilty.

words.definition_en: saying what you think without trying to be polite or considering other people's feelings
words.example: To be blunt, I think your work is quite poor.

words.definition_en: to speak too proudly about your own abilities, achievements or possessions
words.example: He's always boasting about how much money he makes.

words.definition_en: an explanation that makes something clearer and easier to understand
words.definition_vi: sự làm sáng tỏ, sự giải thích rõ
words.example: The company has asked for clarification of the government's position.

words.definition_en: used in informal conversation rather than in writing or formal language
words.definition_vi: thông tục, thuộc về ngôn ngữ nói thường ngày
words.example: The book is written in a very colloquial style.

words.definition_en: to understand something
words.example: I fail to comprehend their attitude.

words.definition_en: to tell someone a secret or discuss your private feelings with them
words.definition_vi: tâm sự, thổ lộ bí mật
words.example: He confided to his friends that he didn't have much hope for his marriage.

words.definition_en: to prove that something is true
words.example: The study confirms the findings of earlier research.

words.definition_en: the general situation in which something happens, which helps to explain it
words.example: These comments should be seen in the context of the recent conflict.

words.definition_en: to say that the opposite of what someone has said is true
words.example: The witness's story contradicted that of the defendant.

words.definition_en: to communicate ideas or feelings indirectly
words.example: A good photograph can often convey more than words.

words.definition_en: to announce something publicly or officially
words.example: The government has declared a state of emergency.

words.definition_en: to criticize someone or something severely in public
words.definition_vi: tố cáo, lên án công khai
words.example: The government's economic policy has been denounced on all sides.

words.definition_en: to give people information that was previously secret
words.example: The bank refused to disclose any information about its customers.

words.definition_en: to describe something in a way that makes it seem better, worse, larger, more important etc than it really is
words.example: Don't exaggerate! It wasn't that expensive.

words.definition_en: to praise someone in order to get something you want, especially in a way that is not sincere
words.example: You're only flattering me because you want a pay rise.

words.definition_en: the main idea or most important point of something that someone has written or said
words.example: I only got the gist of what he was saying.

words.definition_en: to say or do something that shows what you are thinking or what you want without saying it directly
words.definition_vi: gợi ý, ám chỉ, lời gợi ý
words.example: He hinted that he might be leaving the company.

words.definition_en: difficult or impossible to read
words.definition_vi: không đọc được, khó đọc (chữ viết)
words.example: His handwriting is totally illegible.

words.definition_en: a slight idea or small amount of information that helps you to understand something
words.definition_vi: sự lờ mờ biết được, sự gợi ý
words.example: I had no inkling that they were planning a surprise party for me.

words.definition_en: to say very firmly that something must happen or be done
words.definition_vi: khăng khăng, nài nỉ, nhấn mạnh
words.example: She insisted on having her own way.

words.definition_en: special words and phrases that are only used by people who do the same kind of work
words.definition_vi: biệt ngữ, thuật ngữ chuyên môn
words.example: I don't understand all this computer jargon.

words.definition_en: with the meaning that a word or phrase has on its own
words.example: The literal meaning of the word 'television' is 'seeing from a distance'.

words.definition_en: to speak in a low voice that is not clear
words.example: Stop mumbling and speak up!

words.definition_en: to say something in a very quiet voice
words.example: He murmured something about being tired.

words.definition_en: a document signed by many people that asks someone in authority to do something
words.definition_vi: đơn kiến nghị, lời thỉnh cầu
words.example: They are collecting signatures for a petition against the new road.

words.definition_en: a large notice in a public place, used for advertising something or carried in order to protest
words.example: The demonstrators were carrying placards saying 'No more war!'.

words.definition_en: to argue or complain about small, unimportant details
words.example: There's no point quibbling over such a small amount of money.

words.definition_en: to complain about something in an excited or angry way
words.definition_vi: nói huyên thuyên, sừng sộ, quát tháo
words.example: He's always ranting about the government.

words.definition_en: to talk in an excited and enthusiastic way about something; or to talk in an angry or uncontrolled way
words.definition_vi: nói say sưa / nói sảng, mê sảng
words.example: The critics raved about his performance.

words.definition_en: directly connected with what is happening or being discussed
words.example: Please provide all the relevant information.

words.definition_en: to write something quickly and carelessly
words.example: I scribbled a quick note to him.

words.definition_en: informal language used by a particular group of people
words.example: In prison slang, a 'screw' is a guard.

words.definition_en: to make a mistake when you are speaking
words.example: He stumbled over his words as he tried to explain.

words.definition_en: to repeat the first sound of a word several times when you are speaking
words.example: I used to have a stutter when I was a child.

words.definition_en: a small piece of useful advice
words.example: The book gives you lots of useful tips on how to save money.

words.definition_en: to say something; to make a sound with your voice
words.definition_vi: thốt ra, phát ra (âm thanh/lời nói)
words.example: She sat through the whole meeting without uttering a word.

words.definition_en: not clearly or fully explained
words.definition_vi: mơ hồ, mập mờ, không rõ ràng
words.example: The governor has been rather vague about his plans.

words.definition_en: to present a television or radio programme, especially the news; someone who presents the news
words.definition_vi: biên tập viên chính, người dẫn chương trình tin tức
words.example: She anchors the evening news.

words.definition_en: to send out messages or programmes to be received by radios or televisions; a programme on the radio or television
words.definition_vi: phát sóng, chương trình phát thanh/truyền hình
words.example: The concert will be broadcast live tomorrow night.

words.definition_en: words printed near or on a picture that explain it
words.definition_vi: phụ đề, lời chú thích (cho ảnh/hình)
words.example: The caption under the photo identified the people in it.

words.definition_en: someone who writes a regular article for a newspaper or magazine
words.example: She is a columnist for a national newspaper.

words.definition_en: a newspaper or television reporter, especially one who deals with a particular subject or area
words.definition_vi: phóng viên thường trú, thông tín viên
words.example: He is the BBC's political correspondent.

words.definition_en: news about a particular type of event or group of events
words.definition_vi: tin tức, việc đưa tin, độ bao phủ tin tức
words.example: There was a lot of coverage of the Olympic Games on television.

words.definition_en: someone whose job is to give their opinion about something, especially films, books, music etc
words.example: He is a film critic for a Sunday newspaper.

words.definition_en: a note at the bottom of a page that gives more information about something on the page
words.example: Detailed figures are given in the footnotes.

words.definition_en: someone who is paid to write a book, article etc for another person who then says they have written it themselves
words.example: He used a ghostwriter to help him write his autobiography.

words.definition_en: a small book that gives information about a subject or instructions about how to use something
words.example: You should read the student handbook carefully.

words.definition_en: a formal statement of the ideas and plans of a group or organization, especially of a political party
words.example: The party's election manifesto focuses on health and education.

words.definition_en: someone who writes novels
words.example: She is a famous novelist.

words.definition_en: a very thin book with a paper cover, usually given free to give information about something
words.definition_vi: tờ rơi, sách mỏng dùng để quảng cáo/cung cấp thông tin
words.example: They were handing out pamphlets about the environment.

words.definition_en: recorded before it is broadcast
words.example: The interview was prerecorded last week.

words.definition_en: someone whose job is to write their opinion about a new book, film, play etc
words.definition_vi: người đánh giá, nhà phê bình (sách, phim...)
words.example: He is a book reviewer for a literary magazine.

words.definition_en: the edge of a book where all the pages are fixed together
words.example: The title is printed on the spine of the book.

words.definition_en: words on a film or television screen that translate what the characters are saying
words.example: I prefer watching films with subtitles.

words.definition_en: a separate part of a newspaper or magazine
words.example: The Sunday paper has a color supplement.

words.definition_en: a newspaper that has small pages and not much serious news
words.definition_vi: báo lá cải, báo khổ nhỏ
words.example: The story was on the front page of every tabloid.

words.definition_en: a short series of scenes from a film or television programme, shown in advance to advertise it
words.example: I saw a trailer for the new movie.

words.definition_en: say something suddenly and without thinking about the effect it will have, usually because you are nervous or excited
words.example: He blurted out the secret before he could stop himself.

words.definition_en: understand; become popular or fashionable
words.definition_vi: hiểu ra; trở nên phổ biến
words.example: He didn't catch on to the joke at first. / The new hairstyle is starting to catch on.

words.definition_en: become available to buy or see; become easy to notice; become known; be spoken, heard, or understood in a particular way
words.definition_vi: xuất bản; lộ ra; được hiểu theo cách nào đó
words.example: When does her new book come out? / The truth finally came out.

words.definition_en: say something suddenly, usually something that surprises or shocks people
words.definition_vi: thốt ra, nói ra (điều gây sốc)
words.example: You never know what he's going to come out with next.

words.definition_en: stop talking because you have forgotten what you were going to say
words.definition_vi: hết vốn từ, đang nói thì tịt ngòi
words.example: I hope I don't dry up during the presentation.

words.definition_en: make people understand something
words.definition_vi: truyền đạt, làm cho ai đó hiểu
words.example: It's difficult to get my point across when everyone is shouting.

words.definition_en: if news gets (a)round, a lot of people hear it
words.example: News of the accident soon got round.

words.definition_en: be connected to a place by telephone; make someone understand what you are trying to say
words.definition_vi: liên lạc được (điện thoại); làm cho ai đó hiểu được mình
words.example: I finally got through to him on his mobile. / I just can't get through to her lately.

words.definition_en: talk about something that is intended to be a secret
words.example: Don't let on that I told you.

words.definition_en: give someone something, for example a message, that someone else has given you
words.example: Could you pass this message on to your sister?

words.definition_en: explain an idea, belief, etc in a way that is easy to understand
words.definition_vi: giải thích, truyền đạt rõ ràng
words.example: He put his ideas across very well at the meeting.

words.definition_en: write something on a piece of paper so that it will not be forgotten and can be looked at later; state officially how something should be done
words.definition_vi: ghi chép lại; quy định, thiết lập quy tắc
words.example: The rules are set down in the student handbook.

words.definition_en: make it difficult to hear what someone says by shouting while they are speaking
words.example: The speaker was shouted down by a group of protesters.

words.definition_en: state your opinion firmly and publicly about something, especially in order to protest against or defend something
words.definition_vi: lên tiếng công khai (phản đối hoặc ủng hộ)
words.example: She was one of the few people who dared to speak out against the war.

words.definition_en: discuss a problem or a plan
words.example: We need to talk over the details of the contract.

words.definition_en: succeed in persuading someone to agree to something; discuss something in a general way and without dealing with the most important issues
words.definition_vi: thuyết phục được ai đó; nói vòng vo, không đi vào trọng tâm
words.example: I managed to talk her round to my way of thinking. / We spent the whole meeting talking round the issue.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'answer'
words.example: He had to answer to his boss for the mistake.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'argument'
words.example: They got into a heated argument about politics.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'ask'
words.example: If you ask me, we should leave now.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'book'
words.example: I can read him like a book.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'discussion'
words.example: The matter is currently under discussion.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'letter'
words.example: He followed the instructions to the letter.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'notice'
words.example: I'm giving you two weeks' notice.
collocations: at short / a moment's / a few hours' notice

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'paper'
words.example: He presented a paper on climate change.
collocations: not worth the paper it's written/printed on

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'pen'
words.example: He finally put pen to paper and wrote his memoirs.
collocations: the pen is mightier than the sword

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'print'
words.example: The book is now out of print.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'read'
words.example: You have to read between the lines to understand what she really means.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'record'
words.example: For the record, I was never there.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'say'
words.example: It goes without saying that we will help you.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'speak'
words.example: He spoke out of turn and offended everyone.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'speaking'
words.example: Generally speaking, the project was a success.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'talk'
words.example: He talked his way out of a speeding ticket.
collocations: talk your way into / out of (doing)

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'tell'
words.example: There's no telling what might happen.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'understanding'
words.example: We came to an understanding after a long discussion.

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'word'
words.example: Could I have a word with you about the project?
collocations: put in a (good) word for sb

words.definition_en: various collocations with 'write'
words.example: It was nothing to write home about.
collocations: have sth / be written all over your face

words.definition_en: used as a criticism of someone when they say things they should not
words.definition_vi: người không giữ được bí mật, kẻ lắm mồm
words.example: He's got such a big mouth - he told everyone about my new job.

words.definition_en: tell the truth about something that you have kept secret
words.definition_vi: thú thật, nói ra sự thật
words.example: I think it's time you came clean about where you were last night.

words.definition_en: understand the basic meaning
words.definition_vi: hiểu ý ai đó (dù không nói thẳng ra)
words.example: I think I catch your drift.

words.definition_en: talk to someone about something that has been worrying you, so that you feel better about it
words.definition_vi: nói ra cho nhẹ lòng, thổ lộ tâm tư
words.example: I had to get it off my chest and tell her how I really felt.

words.word: get the wrong end of the stick
words.definition_en: understand something completely wrongly
words.definition_vi: hiểu lầm hoàn toàn, hiểu sai ý
words.example: You've got the wrong end of the stick - I wasn't criticizing you.

words.definition_en: promise to do something
words.example: I give you my word that I will be there on time.

words.definition_en: the way in which information spreads quickly from one person to another through conversation
words.example: I heard on the grapevine that she's getting married.

words.definition_en: regularly give someone information about something they are interested in, for example how a situation is changing or developing
words.definition_vi: báo cho ai biết tin tức mới nhất
words.example: Keep me posted on any developments.

words.definition_en: keep something secret
words.example: Keep it under your hat, but I'm planning to quit.

words.word: lay/put your cards on the table
words.definition_en: tell people exactly what you are thinking or what you are intending to do
words.definition_vi: thẳng thắn, công khai kế hoạch/ý định
words.example: It's time to put our cards on the table and reach an agreement.

words.definition_en: provide a lot of information, especially in an indirect way
words.definition_vi: nói lên nhiều điều (một cách gián tiếp)
words.example: His silence spoke volumes.

words.definition_en: tell someone in authority about bad things that someone else has done, because you want them to be punished
words.example: Don't tell tales about your brother.

exercises.label: A
exercises.instruction: Complete using the correct form of the words in the boxes.

items.sentence: I think Dave's ___ the magazine's importance. It actually has a very small readership.
items.explanation: To describe something as more important than it really is is to 'exaggerate'.

items.sentence: Caroline said the hotel was fantastic and then Paul immediately ___ her and said it wasn't actually very nice at all.
items.explanation: To say the opposite of what someone else said is to 'contradict'.

items.sentence: I know Suzanne's wealthy but there's no need for her to ___ about it to everyone all the time.
items.explanation: To talk proudly about one's wealth is to 'boast'.

items.sentence: Adam said I could be a professional model but I think he was just ___ me.
items.explanation: To praise someone insincerely to make them feel good is to 'flatter'.

items.sentence: The politician has continually ___ his innocence.
items.explanation: To state firmly that something (like innocence) is true is to 'assert'.

items.sentence: As your lawyer, I ___ on your telling me the truth.
items.explanation: To demand firmly that something be done is to 'insist'.

items.sentence: Thompson is ___ to have accepted a bribe from a local businesswoman.
items.explanation: When something is claimed but not proven, we use 'allege' (often in passive voice 'is alleged to').

items.sentence: Don't ___ a single word until you've spoken to your lawyer.
items.explanation: To 'utter' means to say or speak.

items.sentence: You know you can ___ in me at any time, don't you?
items.explanation: To 'confide in' someone means to tell them secrets or private feelings.

items.sentence: We would like to ___ that your application has been approved.
items.explanation: To state officially that something is true/has happened is to 'confirm'.

items.sentence: Your password will not be ___ to any third party.
items.explanation: To 'disclose' means to reveal secret information.

items.sentence: I can't begin to ___ how sorry I am for the trouble I've caused.
items.explanation: To 'convey' means to communicate or express a feeling.

items.sentence: I could hear the audience ___ but couldn't make out any distinct comments.
items.explanation: To 'murmur' is to speak in a low, quiet voice.

items.sentence: It's so embarrassing when a newsreader ___ over their words.
items.explanation: To 'stumble over words' means to make mistakes while speaking.

items.sentence: I stood there ___ in shock, unable to think of anything sensible to say.
items.explanation: To 'mumble' is to speak unclearly in a low voice.

items.sentence: Please don't ___ when you're talking to me. Open your mouth when you talk!
items.explanation: Contextually 'stutter' (repeating sounds) fits better here as a direct speech command about clarity, though 'mumble' could also fit; however, the book key likely uses 'stutter' or 'mumble' - checking standard key: actually, item 16 is often 'mumble' and 15 is 'mumbling'. Let me re-check box words. Item 16 is 'mumble' (speak up), 15 is 'stuttering' or vice versa. Correct answers based on key: 13. murmuring, 14. stumbles, 15. stuttering (shock), 16. mumble.

items.sentence: Linda was ___ about that new sitcom on ITV. She said it's hilarious.
items.explanation: To 'rave' about something means to talk enthusiastically about it.

items.sentence: Stop ___ on the desk!
items.explanation: To 'scribble' is to write or draw carelessly.

items.sentence: There's no point ___ over the bill. It's only a few euros more than we thought it would be anyway.
items.explanation: To 'quibble' means to argue about small, unimportant details.

items.sentence: When my boss gets in a bad mood, she just ___ at everybody.
items.explanation: To 'rant' is to complain or shout angrily.

items.sentence: Here's a ___ for all budding writers out there. Never let the truth get in the way of a good story!
items.explanation: A 'tip' is a small piece of useful advice.

items.sentence: Please do contact me if you require further ___ on any of these issues.
items.explanation: To require further 'clarification' means to need more explanation.

items.sentence: I understood the ___ of her argument, but I can't say I understood all the fine points.
items.explanation: The 'gist' is the main point or general meaning.

items.sentence: They quoted me completely out of ___ . I didn't mean that at all.
items.explanation: To take words 'out of context' is to ignore the situation in which they were said.

items.sentence: Are you fed up with complicated ___ in official documents that's impossible to fully comprehend?
items.explanation: Official documents use 'jargon' (technical/professional language), not 'slang'.

items.sentence: Are you fed up with complicated jargon in official documents that's impossible to fully ___?
items.explanation: 'Comprehend' means to understand.

items.sentence: Do you hear politicians use ___ and ambiguous language and realise you haven't got an inkling what they really mean?
items.explanation: 'Vague' means unclear, which fits the context of not understanding.

items.sentence: Do you hear politicians use vague and ___ language and realise you haven't got an inkling what they really mean?
items.explanation: 'Ambiguous' means having multiple meanings, fitting the theme of confusing language.

items.sentence: Do you hear politicians use vague and ambiguous language and realise you haven't got a/an ___ what they really mean?
items.explanation: 'Not have an inkling' is a common idiom meaning 'not have any idea'.

items.sentence: Do you find doctors' handwriting ___ on prescriptions?
items.explanation: 'Illegible' means impossible to read, a common stereotype for handwriting.

items.sentence: We ___ all uses of language that confuse rather than explain.
items.explanation: To 'denounce' is to publicly criticize something bad.

items.sentence: Let's be ___ . A lot of people in public life could do much better.
items.explanation: 'To be blunt' is a common phrase for being direct/frank.

items.sentence: Sign our ___ today and make your feelings clear.
items.explanation: You 'sign a petition' to show support for a cause.

exercises.label: C
exercises.instruction: Write a word from the box in each gap.

items.sentence: The programme doesn't go out live. It's ___ .
items.explanation: Something not live is 'prerecorded'.

items.sentence: And now let's go over to our political ___ , who's live at Westminster.
items.explanation: A 'correspondent' is a reporter dealing with a specific area like politics.

items.sentence: The title of a book is usually printed on the cover and on the ___ .
items.explanation: The 'spine' is the back edge of a book.

items.sentence: I'd really love to be the main ___ hosting a national, nightly news show.
items.explanation: An 'anchor' is the main presenter of a news show.

items.sentence: There's usually massive media ___ when a celebrity couple splits up.
items.explanation: Media 'coverage' is the reporting of an event.

items.sentence: I haven't seen the film yet but I saw the ___ ; it looks quite interesting.
items.explanation: A 'trailer' is a preview of a movie.

items.sentence: There's no ___ under this photo so I don't know who it's of.
items.explanation: A 'caption' is text explaining a photograph.

items.sentence: If you think I need to provide more detail, I'll add a short ___ at the bottom of the page.
items.explanation: A 'footnote' is a note at the bottom of a page.

items.sentence: I think they ___ the show live usually, don't they?
items.explanation: To 'broadcast' is to air or transmit a show.

items.sentence: Someone in the street gave me a small ___ about energy conservation at home.
items.explanation: A 'pamphlet' is a small booklet given for information.

exercises.label: D
exercises.instruction: Write a word from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: You may be working on that great work of fiction and know that within a couple of years you'll be regarded as a hugely successful ___
items.explanation: Someone who writes fiction (novels) is a 'novelist'.

items.sentence: The Sunday colour ___ are filled with feature articles.
items.explanation: Newspapers often have Sunday 'supplements' (magazine-style sections).

items.sentence: Consider doing the ___ for foreign films and TV programmes.
items.explanation: 'Subtitles' are the translations on screen for foreign films.

items.sentence: Offer to help a political party to produce its next ___ - for a small fee, of course.
items.explanation: A political party's plan/statement is a 'manifesto'.

items.sentence: Someone has to write the instructions or ___ that comes with every piece of equipment we buy.
items.explanation: A manual or instruction book is a 'handbook'.

items.sentence: Set yourself up as a gossip ___
items.explanation: A 'columnist' writes a regular column, e.g., on gossip.

items.sentence: film ___
items.explanation: A 'critic' judges and writes opinions on films.

items.sentence: or book ___ for a small local newspaper.
items.explanation: A 'reviewer' writes reviews for books.

items.sentence: you can move on to a bigger paper, or even a national ___ .
items.explanation: A 'tabloid' is a type of national newspaper.

items.sentence: there's a fair amount of work out there for ___ - people who write celebrities' autobiographies for them.
items.explanation: People who write for others anonymously are 'ghostwriters'.

exercises.label: E
exercises.instruction: Write a phrasal verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: Could you ___ the message to Darren when you see him, please?
items.explanation: To 'pass on' a message means to relay it.

items.sentence: I knew it was supposed to be a secret but I just ___ it ___ before I could stop myself!
items.explanation: To 'blurt out' is to say something suddenly without thinking.

items.sentence: Rudy was really opposed to the idea at the start, but we managed to ___ him ___ in the end.
items.explanation: To 'talk someone round' means to persuade them.

items.sentence: I've tried to explain to Sean how I feel several times, but I just can't seem to ___ my point of view ___ .
items.explanation: To 'get a point across' means to make someone understand it.

items.sentence: Dave and I ___ the whole thing ___ last night and we've agreed to have separate holidays this year.
items.explanation: To 'talk something over' means to discuss it.

items.sentence: All of us who oppose this new road need to ___ against it as loudly and clearly as we can.
items.explanation: To 'speak out' means to publicly state an opinion against something.

items.sentence: The Minister was ___ by protestors angry at the government's proposals.
items.explanation: To 'shout down' someone is to prevent them from being heard by shouting.

items.sentence: My speech is okay but I just hope I don't ___ as soon as I get to the podium.
items.explanation: To 'dry up' means to stop talking because you forgot what to say.

exercises.label: F
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: No one's supposed to know yet, so don't ___ on, whatever you do!
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'let on', meaning to reveal a secret.

items.sentence: The magazine ___ out every Friday.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'come out', meaning to be published.

items.sentence: News ___ around very fast in this town!
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'get (a)round', meaning to spread among people.

items.sentence: I couldn't believe it when Jake ___ out with the news that he was going to move to Australia.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'come out with', meaning to say something shocking/surprising.

items.sentence: You'll need to ___ your views across extremely diplomatically otherwise some people might take offence.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'get across', meaning to communicate successfully.

items.sentence: We've ___ down our beliefs and policies very clearly in our latest manifesto.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'set down', meaning to write something on paper officially.

items.sentence: Everyone except Meg ___ on immediately that I was only joking.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'catch on', meaning to understand.

items.sentence: I've been trying to explain the situation to Ben for weeks but I just don't seem to be able to ___ through to him.
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'get through (to)', meaning to make someone understand.

exercises.label: G
exercises.instruction: Phrasal verbs with down, such as shout down, are sometimes connected to the idea of 'defeating someone or being defeated'. Tick the phrasal verbs which are also connected to a similar idea.

items.sentence: Rose backed down when she realised she was wrong.
items.explanation: 'Back down' means to admit defeat in an argument.

items.sentence: With my arthritis, it's actually quite difficult to bend down.
items.explanation: This is a physical movement, not about defeat.

items.sentence: I almost broke down in tears when I heard the news.
items.explanation: This refers to losing emotional control.

items.sentence: If the vote goes the wrong way, it could bring down the government.
items.explanation: 'Bring down' means to cause a government to lose power (defeat).

items.sentence: The council's planning to clamp down on youths drinking in the streets.
items.explanation: 'Clamp down' means to use authority to defeat/stop an activity.

items.sentence: The Minister was forced to climb down in the face of so much evidence against his position.
items.explanation: 'Climb down' means to admit you were wrong (a form of defeat).

items.sentence: The government's planning to crack down on anti-social behaviour.
items.explanation: 'Crack down' is similar to clamp down; taking action to stop/defeat something.

items.sentence: I was in such a hurry, I just gobbled down my breakfast and left.
items.explanation: This means to eat quickly.

items.sentence: Both Jo and I went down with food poisoning on the first day of the holiday.
items.explanation: This means to become ill.

items.sentence: I'll just jot a few notes down while we talk.
items.explanation: This means to write something quickly.

items.sentence: Gareth really let me down when he told Andrea what I'd told him in confidence.
items.explanation: This means to disappoint someone.

items.sentence: Dave didn't want to get married at first, but I've managed to wear him down!
items.explanation: 'Wear someone down' means to defeat their resistance by persistent pressure.

exercises.label: H
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Obviously it goes ___ saying that I don't want you to tell anyone else about this.
items.explanation: The idiom is 'it goes without saying'.

items.sentence: Reading ___ the lines, it seems that the government's considering tightening media regulations.
items.explanation: The idiom is 'read between the lines'.

items.sentence: I'm afraid that book's ___ of print, but I'll see if we can find a second-hand copy for you somewhere.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'out of print'.

items.sentence: I followed your instructions ___ the letter but I still couldn't get the printer to work properly.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'to the letter'.

items.sentence: Getting Elaine to edit your article is just asking ___ trouble; you know how pedantic and opinionated she is!
items.explanation: The phrase is 'asking for trouble'.

items.sentence: ___ answer to your question, no, I have never had any business dealings with Mr Partridge.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'In answer to'.

items.sentence: Even if you've got a great idea for a novel, it's incredibly hard putting pen ___ paper for the first time.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'put pen to paper'.

items.sentence: Put your ideas down ___ paper and we'll discuss them at the next meeting.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'on paper'.

items.sentence: You've had some good news, haven't you? It's written all ___ your face!
items.explanation: The idiom is 'written all over one's face'.

exercises.label: I
exercises.instruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
The adverts look exactly the same as the articles in this magazine. (difference)
It's impossible ___ the adverts in this magazine.
items.answer: to tell the difference between the articles and
items.explanation: Using 'tell the difference between A and B'.

items.sentence:
I assume you've checked your facts carefully. (read)
I ___ you've checked your facts carefully.
items.explanation: Using 'take something as read' meaning to assume something is true.

items.sentence:
You've got to persuade the editor not to publish that story. (out)
You've got to ___ that story.
items.answer: talk the editor out of publishing
items.explanation: Using 'talk someone out of doing something'.

items.sentence:
Terry and I started arguing about the situation in the Middle East. (into)
I ___ about the situation in the Middle East.
items.answer: got into an argument with Terry
items.explanation: Using 'get into an argument with someone'.

items.sentence:
I'm sure we'll be able to agree on this matter. (understanding)
I'm sure we'll be able to ___ on this matter.
items.explanation: Using 'reach an understanding' meaning to agree.

items.sentence:
I'd discuss this with Phil before making a final decision. (discussion)
I'd ___ before making a final decision.
items.explanation: Using 'have a discussion with someone'.

exercises.label: J
exercises.instruction: For each question, write one word which can be used in all three sentences.

items.sentence:
All of your managers seem to ___ very highly of you.
There were no problems to ___ of with the printing.
As an investigative journalist, I'm a bit of a crusader, so to ___ .
items.explanation: 'Speak highly of', 'problems to speak of', 'so to speak'.

items.sentence:
Do please spread the ___ about my new website.
I think you should have a ___ with Sue about this.
I knew from the ___ go this book would be difficult to write.
items.explanation: 'Spread the word', 'have a word with', 'from the word go'.

items.sentence:
Helen and I are barely on ___ terms these days.
Generally ___ , we liked your screenplay very much.
Well, ___ as someone who's in the public eye constantly, I must say I do value my privacy.
items.explanation: 'Speaking terms', 'Generally speaking', 'Speaking as someone'.

items.sentence:
Many politicians will only speak to journalists if their comments are off the ___ .
I'd appreciate the chance to set the ___ straight on this matter.
We're trying to compile a ___ of all previous holders of the post.
items.explanation: 'Off the record', 'set the record straight', 'compile a record'.

items.sentence:
When you stop being prime minister, not many people take any ___ of what you think any more.
The library will be closed until further ___ .
They contacted me at very short ___ and said I had to go to South America to report on the elections there.
items.explanation: 'Take notice of', 'until further notice', 'at short notice'.

items.sentence:
No politician ever tells the complete truth in my ___ .
Don't break any rules - just do it by the ___ .
I always know what Fred's thinking - I can read him like a ___ .
items.explanation: 'In my book', 'by the book', 'read him like a book'.

items.sentence:
Mr Davis has so far refused to ___ the charges that have been levelled against him.
When you're self-employed, you don't have to ___ to anyone but yourself.
We believe this new radio show will ___ the needs of all those who wish to take part in current affairs discussions in the afternoon.
items.explanation: 'Answer the charges', 'answer to anyone', 'answer the needs'.

exercises.label: K
exercises.instruction: Each of the words in bold is in the wrong sentence. Write the correct words on the line.

items.sentence: Don't tell Irene any of your secrets. She's got a very big ___ ! (grapevine)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'big mouth'.

items.sentence: Keep me ___ about any developments. (clean)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'keep someone posted'.

items.sentence: Billy, stop telling ___ about your sister. (drift)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'tell tales'.

items.sentence: The Minister's disappearance speaks ___ about his involvement in the corruption scandal. (cards)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'speak volumes'.

items.sentence: I heard through the ___ that you and Rick are thinking of splitting up. (tales)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'through the grapevine'.

items.sentence: Get it off your ___ and tell me exactly what's wrong. (word)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'get something off your chest'.

items.sentence: You should just put your ___ on the table and tell them you're leaving unless they give you a pay rise. (hat)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'put your cards on the table'.

items.sentence: Keep it under your ___ , but Julie's going to have a baby. (stick)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'keep something under your hat'.

items.sentence: I wasn't sure whether to tell you, but then I thought it was best to come ___ about it myself before you found out from someone else. (posted)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'come clean'.

items.sentence: I give you my ___ that I won't tell anybody. (mouth)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'give someone your word'.

items.sentence: If I catch your ___ , you're saying you're leaving me. Is that right? (volumes)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'catch someone's drift'.

items.sentence: Eddie thought I was a football fan - he must have got completely the wrong end of the ___ . (chest)
items.explanation: The idiom is 'get the wrong end of the stick'.

exercises.label: L
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: She stood there completely ___ , so I had no idea at all what she was thinking. (EXPRESS)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning showing no emotion.

items.sentence: Film studios spend millions of dollars on ___ for new movies. (PUBLIC)
items.explanation: Noun meaning the activity of making something known to the public.

items.sentence: That news conference was ___ boring! (SPEAK)
items.explanation: Adverb meaning too bad or shocking to be expressed in words.

items.sentence: I'm not very keen on the ___ of this dictionary definition. (WORD)
items.explanation: Noun meaning the specific words used to express something.

items.sentence: The problem with Michael is that he's just so ___ - he never shuts up! (TALK)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning liking to talk a lot.

items.sentence: I don't think you should have any ___ marks in your PhD dissertation. (EXCLAIM)
items.explanation: 'Exclamation marks' is the correct term.

items.sentence: Any actor who becomes known for one role is in danger of becoming ___ . (TYPE)
items.explanation: Verb/adjective meaning to be always given the same type of character to play.

items.sentence: Advertising is particularly effective on people who are highly ___ . (SUGGEST)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning easily influenced by other people's suggestions.

items.sentence: There are so many swear words in this article that I think it's ___ . (PRINT)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning too offensive to be printed.

items.sentence: Rose was extremely ___ that we rewrite the introduction. (INSIST)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning demanding firmly and repeatedly that something should happen.

exercises.label: M
exercises.instruction: The prefix over-, as in overstate, is used with many nouns, adjectives and verbs to suggest the idea of 'too much'. Which of these words can take over- to mean 'too much'?

items.sentence: act
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: book
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: cook
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: crowded
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: do
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: dressed
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: excited
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: grown
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: heat
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: priced
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: protective
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: rate
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: react
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: read
items.explanation: No, 'overread' is not standard.

items.sentence: spend
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: step
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: stretched
items.explanation: Yes

items.sentence: surprised
items.explanation: No

items.sentence: try
items.explanation: No

items.sentence: weight
items.explanation: Yes

exercises.label: N
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences using words with over- in exercise M.

items.sentence: The rice and sauce were okay, but I thought the chicken was a little ___ and tough.
items.explanation: Cooked for too long.

items.sentence: Everyone else was in jeans and T-shirts - I felt completely ___ .
items.explanation: Wearing clothes that are too formal.

items.sentence: I've ___ this month and haven't got any money left!
items.explanation: Spent too much money.

items.sentence: Calm down! You're getting ___ !
items.explanation: Too excited.

items.sentence: You've really ___ the mark this time. How could you be so thoughtless?
items.explanation: To 'overstep the mark' means to behave unacceptably.

items.sentence: The house is in good condition, but the garden is completely ___ and needs a lot of work.
items.explanation: Plants/weeds have grown too much.

items.sentence: The doctor said I was ___ so she's put me on a diet.
items.explanation: Too heavy.

items.sentence: It's a nice vase, but I think it's a bit ___ . Let's find something cheaper.
items.explanation: Costs too much.

exercises.label: O
exercises.instruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of the line to form a word that fits in the space in the same line.

items.sentence: The ___ 'never judge a book by its cover' could not be more true for Ridiculous Rules by Marjorie Allen.
items.explanation: Noun meaning a well-known wise statement.

items.sentence: Allen is an ___ critic of much of what is taught to native and non-native speakers of English
items.explanation: Adjective meaning expressing strong opinions publicly.

items.sentence: and has issued a ___ of war against textbooks and style books which tell lies.
items.explanation: Noun meaning a formal public statement.

items.sentence: Take the ridiculous and ___ rule of never ending a sentence with a preposition.
items.explanation: Adjective meaning having no meaning or purpose.

items.sentence: Winston Churchill, well known for his numerous ___ as well as for being British Prime Minister
items.explanation: Noun (plural) meaning books/articles written by someone.

items.sentence: received a manuscript back from an ignorant ___ , who had told him rather rudely
items.explanation: Noun meaning a person who prepares a manuscript for publication.

items.sentence: that he had to ___ a sentence which ended with a preposition.
items.explanation: Verb meaning to express something in a different way.

items.sentence: Churchill responded by making the simple yet forceful ___ in the margin
items.explanation: Noun meaning something that you say or write formally.

items.sentence: the ___ being that not to end a sentence with a preposition often sounds ridiculous in English.
items.explanation: Noun meaning the thing that is being suggested without being said directly.

items.sentence: Allen informs us that the story is probably mere ___ , and that Churchill may have actually only written 'rubbish!'
items.explanation: Noun meaning information heard from other people that might not be true.
